text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing a large ovarian cyst on the left side. The uterus and sigmoid colon are visible, with the pelvis appearing normal. The surgeon supports the ovarian cyst by pulling the infundibulopelvic ligament with the left hand and uses an aspiration needle to aspirate the cystic content, facilitating its removal.

The surgeon supports the ovarian cyst and uses an aspiration needle to deflate it completely, providing better exposure of the surrounding area. The cyst is gradually separated from the surrounding tissue. Monopolar scissors or a harmonic device are used to cut along the anti-meso-ovarian border.

The surgeon holds the outer ovarian cortex with the left hand while using the right hand to strip the cyst from the surrounding tissue. This stripping technique is repeated multiple times, gradually separating the cyst from the ovarian cortex.
The surgeon gently maneuvers the nearly detached ovarian cyst, facilitating its easy removal from the surgical field.

The surgeon successfully removes the ovarian cyst, revealing a healthy outer ovarian cortex. The uterus appears normal, and the remaining ovarian pocket is inspected for bleeding. Excess tissue is expected to shrink and normalize over time.

The surgeon performs repeated irrigation and suction within the pelvic cavity, ensuring thorough cleaning. Some fluid is intentionally left inside, with the patient's head elevated to direct the fluid towards the pelvis. The omentum and intestines are gently shaken to facilitate fluid distribution.

The surgeon uses a 5mm port at the umbilical site to extract the cyst, which is directly sucked and pulled out through the cannula without using an endo bag, as it is a simple cyst and remains intact. Ringer lactate is used for its anti-adhesive properties.
